ontology.foodex2_description: Cakes, pastries and similar baked products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for walnut brownie; A0CKW (cakes, pastries and similar baked products) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of walnut brownie is approximately 60 g, equivalent to one square piece as typically served in a café setting.

nutrient_source.title: Brownies, prepared from recipe — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 18147)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 18147 (brownies, prepared from recipe) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values are adapted to reflect the walnut-enriched variant; walnut addition increases fat, protein, and energy slightly relative to the base brownie. Per-100g values are derived from the USDA SR Legacy data for standard recipe brownies, which is the closest approved-source match for a classic walnut brownie sold in a café context.

gi_source.title: Chocolate cake, made from packet mix with chocolate frosting — Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283; and Wolever TMS et al. peer-reviewed GI data for chocolate baked goods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for walnut brownie in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 43 is estimated from the closest analogous chocolate baked goods in the University of Sydney GI Database (chocolate cake GI range 38–54; fudge brownie analogues approximately 40–45). A midpoint estimate of 43 is used, classified as low GI. Glycemic load per 60 g serving = (43 × 57.8 × 0.60) / 100 = approximately 14.9, rounded to 14.0. Walnuts have a negligible GI contribution. Estimation basis documented per Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and high sugar load
hypertension.guidance: A 60 g serving of walnut brownie contributes approximately 111 mg sodium and 24 g sugar; individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to occasional treats and monitor total daily sodium and added-sugar intake, as high sugar diets are independently associated with elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar and refined carbohydrate content despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the estimated GI of 43 is in the low range, a 60 g serving delivers approximately 34.7 g total carbohydrate and 24 g sugar, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 14, which is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should treat walnut brownies as an occasional indulgence, pair with protein or fat to blunt postprandial glucose response, and account for the serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and high sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 60 g serving provides approximately 4.3 g saturated fat, 0.1 g trans fat, and 37 mg cholesterol; while walnuts contribute cardioprotective polyunsaturated fatty acids (omega-3 ALA), the overall saturated fat and sugar load means individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit walnut brownie consumption and prioritise whole-food walnut sources for cardiovascular benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A walnut brownie is a dense, fudgy baked chocolate dessert originating in the United States, made by combining melted dark chocolate or cocoa powder with butter, sugar, eggs, a small amount of flour, and chopped walnuts. The batter is poured into a rectangular pan and baked at moderate heat until the centre is just set, producing a characteristically moist, chewy interior with a slightly crackled top crust. The addition of walnuts provides a contrasting crunch and a mild, earthy bitterness that balances the intense sweetness of the chocolate base. Walnut brownies are a staple of Western café menus and are commonly served as a standalone dessert or paired with ice cream or whipped cream. In Singapore, walnut brownies appear on café menus such as The Tree Cafe and are typically sold as individual square portions at a premium price point. Nutritionally, a 60 g serving delivers approximately 244 kcal, 34.7 g carbohydrate (of which 24 g is sugar), 11.1 g fat, and 2.9 g protein. The estimated glycemic index is 43 (low GI), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 14, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Walnuts contribute polyunsaturated fatty acids including alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), an omega-3 precursor, as well as magnesium and antioxidant polyphenols. Clinically, walnut brownies are high in added sugar and saturated fat and should be consumed as an occasional treat by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a walnut brownie?
Is walnut brownie suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of a brownie?
Can I eat brownie if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact match for walnut brownie exists in a primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo) or USDA FoodData Central as a named walnut brownie entry. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 18147 (brownies, prepared from recipe), which is the closest approved-source match. GI is estimated from analogous chocolate baked goods in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for brownies (FDC ID 18147). All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with numeric estimates from the USDA SR Legacy data. Trans fat is present at a low level (0.2 g per 100 g) due to butter content; this is naturally occurring trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid) rather than industrial trans fat. GI of 43 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using chocolate cake and fudge brownie analogues (GI range 38–54); no direct brownie GI measurement was available. Glycemic load per 60 g serving calculated as (43 × 57.8 × 0.60) / 100 ≈ 14.9, reported as 14.0. The food is served at The Tree Cafe - Funan (GrabFood Singapore) at SGD 8.00 per piece. Halal status is assumed based on typical café ingredient sourcing in Singapore but is not certified in the source data; this should be verified with the restaurant. The food_name has been normalised from 'Brownie 🥜' to 'Walnut Brownie' as the canonical English name, with the walnut emoji interpreted as indicating the walnut variant described in the source description.
